days.title: The AI Engineering Mindset
days.linkedinPost:
Most teams treat AI like a magic API call.

That's why 80% of AI projects never reach production.

The AI engineering mindset is different:

→ Start with the problem, not the model
→ Design for failure from day one
→ Measure everything — latency, cost, quality
→ Build eval pipelines before you build features
→ Treat prompts as code — version, test, review

AI engineering isn't about using ChatGPT.

It's about building reliable systems that happen to use LLMs.

What's the biggest challenge you've faced shipping AI to production?
days.imageSuggestion: Diagram showing AI engineering stack: data → model → eval → deploy → monitor

days.title: RAG vs Fine-Tuning
days.linkedinPost:
RAG or fine-tuning? Wrong question.

The right question: What problem are you solving?

Use RAG when:
• Knowledge changes frequently
• You need source attribution
• You want zero training cost
• Domain data is structured (docs, PDFs, wikis)

Use fine-tuning when:
• You need consistent output format
• Style/tone must match your brand
• Task is narrow and well-defined
• You have quality training data (1000+ examples)

In production, most teams use BOTH:
→ Fine-tune for behavior
→ RAG for knowledge

I've seen teams spend $50K on fine-tuning when RAG would have solved it in a week.

Don't over-engineer. Match the tool to the task.

Which approach has worked best for your use case?
days.imageSuggestion: Decision tree: RAG vs Fine-tuning flowchart

days.title: Prompt Engineering in Production
days.linkedinPost:
Prompt engineering in a notebook ≠ prompt engineering in production.

In production, your prompts need:

1. Version control (git, not Google Docs)
2. A/B testing infrastructure
3. Regression tests with golden datasets
4. Fallback chains for model failures
5. Token budget management
6. Structured output validation

Here's my production prompt template:

```
SYSTEM: Role + constraints + output format
CONTEXT: Retrieved docs / user history
TASK: Specific instruction
EXAMPLES: 2-3 few-shot examples
GUARDRAILS: What NOT to do
```

We reduced hallucinations by 40% just by adding explicit guardrails and output schema validation.

Prompts are code. Treat them accordingly.

How do you manage prompts in your team?

days.title: Evaluating LLM Applications
days.linkedinPost:
You can't improve what you can't measure.

Yet most AI teams ship without evals.

Here's a practical eval framework I use:

Level 1 — Unit evals
→ Does the output match the schema?
→ Are required fields present?
→ Is the response within token limits?

Level 2 — Quality evals
→ LLM-as-judge for relevance & accuracy
→ Semantic similarity to golden answers
→ Human review on a sample set

Level 3 — Production evals
→ User feedback signals (thumbs up/down)
→ Task completion rates
→ Latency p95 & cost per request

Start with 50 golden test cases.
Run them on every prompt change.
Block deploys that regress quality.

Evals aren't optional. They're your safety net.

What metrics do you track for your AI products?

days.title: Building AI Agents
days.linkedinPost:
AI agents are having a moment.

But 90% of "agents" are just fancy prompt chains.

A real agent needs:

🧠 Planning — break tasks into steps
🔧 Tools — APIs, databases, code execution
💾 Memory — short-term + long-term context
🔄 Reflection — self-correct on failures
🛡️ Guardrails — budget limits, permission scopes

The architecture that works:

User → Planner → Tool Executor → Evaluator → Response
         ↑                                    ↓
         └──────── retry on failure ──────────┘

Key insight: Keep agents focused.

One agent, one job.

Multi-agent systems add complexity fast. Start with a single agent that does one thing well.

We built a code review agent that handles 200 PRs/day with 92% developer satisfaction.

Are you building agents? What's your architecture?
days.imageSuggestion: Agent loop architecture: plan → act → observe → reflect

days.title: Cost Optimization for LLMs
days.linkedinPost:
Your LLM bill doesn't have to be scary.

We cut inference costs by 65% with these tactics:

1. Model routing
→ GPT-4 for complex tasks
→ Smaller models for classification/extraction
→ Saved 40% alone

2. Prompt compression
→ Remove redundant context
→ Use bullet points over prose
→ Cache system prompts

3. Response caching
→ Semantic cache for similar queries
→ 30% cache hit rate = 30% cost savings

4. Batch processing
→ Non-real-time tasks in batch API
→ 50% discount on most providers

5. Smart truncation
→ Limit context window intelligently
→ Summarize long documents before injection

Track cost per feature, not just total bill.

That one expensive endpoint hiding in your app? You'll find it.

What's your biggest LLM cost optimization win?
days.imageSuggestion: Cost breakdown pie chart with optimization levers

days.title: MLOps for GenAI
days.linkedinPost:
MLOps evolved. GenAI Ops is the next chapter.

Traditional MLOps:
→ Train → Validate → Deploy → Monitor

GenAI Ops adds:
→ Prompt versioning & rollback
→ Embedding pipeline management
→ Vector DB health monitoring
→ LLM provider failover
→ Content safety filtering
→ Cost attribution per user/feature

Your GenAI stack should include:

📦 Observability — LangSmith, Langfuse, or custom
📦 Vector store — Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector
📦 Gateway — LiteLLM or custom router
📦 Eval pipeline — automated regression tests
📦 Feature flags — gradual rollout of prompt changes

The teams winning with GenAI treat it as infrastructure, not a demo.

What's in your GenAI ops stack?
days.imageSuggestion: GenAI Ops pipeline: ingest → embed → retrieve → generate → monitor

days.title: AI Safety & Guardrails
days.linkedinPost:
Shipping AI without guardrails is like deploying without auth.

Essential guardrails for production AI:

🛡️ Input validation
→ Prompt injection detection
→ PII scrubbing before LLM calls
→ Rate limiting per user

🛡️ Output filtering
→ Content moderation API
→ Schema enforcement
→ Confidence thresholds

🛡️ Operational safety
→ Max token budgets per request
→ Timeout & circuit breakers
→ Human-in-the-loop for high-stakes decisions

Real example:

A customer support bot started making up refund policies.

Fix: Constrained generation to approved policy docs + confidence scoring. Escalate to human when confidence < 0.8.

Result: 0 policy hallucinations in 3 months.

Safety isn't a feature. It's a requirement.

How do you handle AI safety in your products?
days.imageSuggestion: Guardrails layers: input → model → output → human review

days.title: The Future of AI Engineering
days.linkedinPost:
5 predictions for AI engineering in 2026-2027:

1️⃣ Small models win
→ Domain-specific 7B models will outperform general 70B+ models on most business tasks

2️⃣ Evals become CI/CD standard
→ Just like unit tests, LLM evals will gate every deployment

3️⃣ AI-native IDEs go mainstream
→ Copilot was just the beginning. Full AI pair programming becomes default

4️⃣ Multimodal becomes default
→ Text-only AI apps will feel outdated. Vision + audio + text is the baseline

5️⃣ AI engineers > prompt engineers
→ The title "prompt engineer" fades. "AI engineer" becomes a core engineering role

The meta-skill that matters most?

Knowing when NOT to use AI.

Not every problem needs an LLM. Sometimes a regex and a database query is the right answer.

Which prediction do you agree with? Which do you think is wrong?
days.imageSuggestion: Timeline infographic of AI engineering evolution

days.title: 10 Lessons from 100 AI Projects
days.linkedinPost:
After 100 AI projects, here are my top 10 lessons:

1. Start with evals, not features
2. RAG beats fine-tuning for 80% of use cases
3. The best model is the cheapest one that works
4. Users don't care about your model — they care about outcomes
5. Prompt changes are breaking changes — version them
6. Latency kills adoption faster than bad quality
7. Build the boring infrastructure first (logging, monitoring, caching)
8. Human-in-the-loop isn't a weakness — it's a feature
9. Demo ≠ product. Plan for edge cases from day one
10. The hardest part isn't the AI — it's the integration

Bonus: Document everything. Future you will thank present you.

This 10-day series covered the fundamentals of AI engineering.

If you found value in these posts, follow for more deep dives on building production AI systems.

Which lesson resonates most with your experience?
days.imageSuggestion: Numbered list graphic with key lessons highlighted
